question: What share of Indians still work in agriculture?

theme.label: Society, work, education, and services
readerQuestions: What basic capability is being measured?
How many people does the percentage represent?
Is this access, usage, quality, or outcome?
Which caveat stops the chart from being overclaimed?

plannedCharts.title: Share of Indians working in agriculture
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline: how much of the workforce is still on the farm.

plannedCharts.title: The slow shift from farm to factory to office
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The classic development path — and how far India still has to go.

plannedCharts.title: The post-2019 reversal
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Recent PLFS data shows agriculture's share rising again — a warning sign.

plannedCharts.title: Half the workers, a fraction of the output
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The core problem: agriculture employs far more people than the value it produces.

plannedCharts.title: Where the economy's value comes from
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The mirror image: services dominate output while agriculture shrinks.

plannedCharts.title: How India compares on leaving the farm
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: China and Vietnam moved workers off farms much faster than India.

plannedCharts.title: Output per worker
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Why moving out of agriculture matters: productivity.

plannedCharts.title: The very long view
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Agriculture's grip on Indian work over more than a century.

plannedCharts.title: Rural work demand: the NREGA signal
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: When farm and non-farm work fall short, rural Indians turn to the jobs guarantee.

plannedCharts.title: Rural wages
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: What rural work actually pays — the floor under farm livelihoods.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
